# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between RSA and DSS digital signature schemes. Mention at least two distinguishing points.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | RSA Signature | DSS / DSA Signature |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Hardness Basis | Integer Factorization Problem | Discrete Logarithm Problem |
| Signature Output | Single integer | Two-integer pair `(r, s)` |
| Hashing | Optional but recommended | Mandatory before signing |
| Speed of Signing | Slower (modular exponentiation to private key) | Faster (uses per-message `k`) |
| Standard Body | PKCS #1 (RSA Labs) | FIPS 186 (NIST) |

*(1 mark for two correct rows, 1 mark for naming hard problem, 1 mark for clarity.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the role of the per-message secret `k` in DSA signing? Why must it be different for every signature?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The per-message secret `k` is a **random ephemeral value** in `[1, q−1]` used to compute `r = (g^k mod p) mod q` and `s = k⁻¹ (H(m) + x·r) mod q`. **(1 mark)**
- It acts as a **one-time blinding factor** that prevents the private key `x` from being directly exposed in the signature, and adds **forward secrecy** to each individual message. **(1 mark)**
- If `k` is **reused for two different messages signed with the same `x`**, an attacker can solve a pair of linear equations to recover `x` (the famous **Sony PS3 ECDSA failure**). Hence `k` must be unique, unpredictable, and ideally generated deterministically per (message, key) pair via RFC 6979. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B carries 14 marks and provides **internal choice**. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. The model below mirrors the standard ESE paper layout.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) in detail. With the help of a block diagram, describe the key generation, signature generation, and signature verification processes.

**Model Solution:**

**Key Generation (3 marks):**
1. Global parameters `(p, q, g)` are chosen as: `p` is a large prime, `q` is a prime divisor of `p−1`, and `g = h^((p−1)/q) mod p` for some `h` such that `g > 1`.
2. User picks a private key `x` with `1 ≤ x ≤ q−1`.
3. User computes public key `y = g^x mod p`.
4. Public key `y` is published; private key `x` is kept secret.

**Signature Generation (2 marks):**
1. Pick a random per-message integer `k` with `1 ≤ k ≤ q−1`.
2. Compute `r = (g^k mod p) mod q`.
3. Compute `s = k⁻¹ · (H(m) + x·r) mod q`, where `H(m)` is the hash of the message.
4. Signature is `(r, s)`. If `r = 0` or `s = 0`, restart with new `k`.

**Signature Verification (2 marks):**
1. Verify `0 < r < q` and `0 < s < q`.
2. Compute `w = s⁻¹ mod q`.
3. Compute `u1 = H(m)·w mod q` and `u2 = r·w mod q`.
4. Compute `v = (g^u1 · y^u2 mod p) mod q`.
5. Accept if and only if `v = r`.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4 diagrams above (printable in the answer booklet). *(Part of the 7-mark allocation is for clean labelled flow.)*

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** In a DSS scheme, the global parameters are `p = 59`, `q = 29`, `g = h^((p−1)/q) mod p`, private key `x = 4`, per-message secret `k = 5`, and message hash `H(m) = 10`. Compute the signature `(r, s)` and verify it. Use `h = 3`.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute `g` (1 mark):**
$z = (p-1)/q = 58/29 = 2$. $g = h^z \bmod p = 3^2 \bmod 59 = 9$.

**Step 2 — Public key (1 mark):**
$y = g^x \bmod p = 9^4 \bmod 59 = (9^2)^2 \bmod 59 = 81^2 \bmod 59 = 22^2 \bmod 59 = 484 \bmod 59 = 484 - 8\cdot59 = 484 - 472 = 12$. So $y = 12$.

**Step 3 — Sign (3 marks):**
- $g^k \bmod p = 9^5 \bmod 59 = 9^4 \cdot 9 = 12 \cdot 9 = 108 \bmod 59 = 108 - 59 = 49$.
- $r = 49 \bmod 29 = 49 - 29 = 20$.
- $k^{-1} \bmod 29$: find $k'$ such that $5k' \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$. $5 \cdot 6 = 30 \equiv 1$. So $k^{-1} = 6$.
- $s = k^{-1} \cdot (H(m) + x \cdot r) \bmod 29 = 6 \cdot (10 + 4 \cdot 20) \bmod 29 = 6 \cdot 90 \bmod 29$.
- $90 \bmod 29 = 90 - 3\cdot29 = 90 - 87 = 3$.
- $s = 6 \cdot 3 \bmod 29 = 18 \bmod 29 = 18$.
- **Signature: $(r, s) = (20, 18)$.**

**Step 4 — Verify (2 marks):**
- $w = s^{-1} \bmod 29$: find $w$ such that $18w \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$. Testing: $18 \cdot 13 = 234 = 8 \cdot 29 + 2$. $18 \cdot 18 = 324 = 11 \cdot 29 + 5$. $18 \cdot 21 = 378 = 13 \cdot 29 + 1$. So $w = 21$.
- $u_1 = H(m) \cdot w \bmod 29 = 10 \cdot 21 \bmod 29 = 210 \bmod 29 = 210 - 7\cdot29 = 210 - 203 = 7$.
- $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod 29 = 20 \cdot 21 \bmod 29 = 420 \bmod 29 = 420 - 14\cdot29 = 420 - 406 = 14$.
- $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod 59) \bmod 29$.
  - $g^{u_1} = 9^7 \bmod 59$. $9^2 = 81 \bmod 59 = 22$. $9^4 = 22^2 = 484 \bmod 59 = 12$. $9^7 = 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9 = 12 \cdot 22 \cdot 9 = 2376 \bmod 59$. $2376 / 59 = 40$ remainder $16$. So $9^7 \equiv 16$.
  - $y^{u_2} = 12^{14} \bmod 59$. $12^2 = 144 \bmod 59 = 144 - 2\cdot59 = 26$. $12^4 = 26^2 = 676 \bmod 59 = 676 - 11\cdot59 = 676 - 649 = 27$. $12^8 = 27^2 = 729 \bmod 59 = 729 - 12\cdot59 = 729 - 708 = 21$. $12^{14} = 12^8 \cdot 12^4 \cdot 12^2 = 21 \cdot 27 \cdot 26 = 14742 \bmod 59$. $14742 / 59 = 249$ remainder $51$. So $12^{14} \equiv 51 \equiv -8 \pmod{59}$.
  - $g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} = 16 \cdot 51 = 816 \bmod 59 = 816 - 13\cdot59 = 816 - 767 = 49$.
  - $v = 49 \bmod 29 = 20$.
- $v = 20 = r$. **Signature VALID ✓**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** List and explain the security requirements that a digital signature scheme must satisfy. How does DSS satisfy each one?

**Model Solution:**

The **four security requirements** of any digital signature scheme are:

1. **Authentication (1 mark):** The signature must verify that the claimed sender actually signed the message. In DSS, the verification equation $v = r$ will succeed *only* if the verifier uses the true sender's public key `y` (since `y = g^x` is mathematically bound to `x`); a forger without `x` cannot produce a valid `(r, s)`.

2. **Integrity (1 mark):** Any modification to the message `m` after signing will change `H(m)`, which feeds into the computation of `s` and `u1`. Since `v` depends on `u1`, the signature verification will fail. DSS guarantees integrity via the **collision-resistant hash** (e.g., SHA-256).

3. **Non-repudiation (1 mark):** The signer cannot later deny having signed. This is because the private key `x` is unique and the signature `(r, s)` could *only* have been generated by someone who knew `x`. Since `x` is solely in the signer's possession, the signer is bound to the signature.

4. **Unforgeability (1 mark):** Without knowing `x`, an adversary cannot produce a valid `(r, s)` for a new message. This relies on the **hardness of the Discrete Logarithm Problem** in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ — given `g` and `y = g^x mod p`, computing `x` is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large `p` (≥ 2048 bits).

**Additional points (3 marks):**
- The **per-message secret `k`** ensures that even if two messages are signed, an attacker cannot combine signatures to extract `x` (assuming `k` is generated with RFC 6979 or a strong CSPRNG).
- DSS also provides **forward secrecy per signature** because `k` is ephemeral.
- Note that DSS does *not* provide **confidentiality** — the message `m` is sent in plaintext; for that, an encryption layer is needed.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Write a Python program (using PyCryptodome) to implement the Digital Signature Standard: generate a 2048-bit DSA key pair, sign the message `"KTU Cryptography Lab"` with SHA-256, and verify the signature. Show the output.

**Model Solution:**

```python
from Crypto.PublicKey import DSA
from Crypto.Signature import DSS
from Crypto.Hash import SHA256

# (1 mark) Key generation
key = DSA.generate(2048)
pub = key.publickey()

# (2 marks) Signing
msg = b"KTU Cryptography Lab"
h = SHA256.new(msg)
signer = DSS.new(key, 'fips-186-3')
sig = signer.sign(h)

# (2 marks) Verification
h2 = SHA256.new(msg)
verifier = DSS.new(pub, 'fips-186-3')
try:
    verifier.verify(h2, sig)
    result = "VALID"
except ValueError:
    result = "INVALID"
print(f"Signature on message '{msg.decode()}' is {result}")

# (1 mark) Tampering test
h_bad = SHA256.new(b"KTU Crypto Lab")
try:
    verifier.verify(h_bad, sig)
    print("Tampered signature VALID (UNEXPECTED!)")
except ValueError:
    print("Tampered message correctly REJECTED")

# (1 mark) Export keys
print("Private key (PEM):\n", key.export_key().decode()[:120], "...")
print("Public key (PEM):\n",  pub.export_key().decode())
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Signature on message 'KTU Cryptography Lab' is VALID
Tampered message correctly REJECTED
Private key (PEM):
 -----BEGIN DSA PRIVATE KEY-----
...
Public key (PEM):
 -----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----
...
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Lab Exam):**
> 1. **Importing wrong classes** — Using `Crypto.Signature.DSS` *before* generating a `DSA` key (not RSA) will throw a `ValueError: No DSA key found`. Always call `DSA.generate(bits)` first.
> 2. **Forgetting the hash** — `DSS.new(key, 'fips-186-3')` requires you to sign a *hash object* (`SHA256.new(msg)`), not the raw message. Passing a string directly will fail.
> 3. **No range check on `r, s`** — In hand-calculated exam questions, students often forget to check `0 < r < q` and `0 < s < q`; **this is mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Inverting a number mod `q` vs mod `p`** — `k⁻¹` and `s⁻¹` are computed **mod `q`**, NOT mod `p`. This is a very common mistake; read the formula sheet carefully.
> 5. **Reusing `k`** — If two signatures share the same `k`, the private key is mathematically leaked. The Sony PS3 hack is the textbook example KTU examiners love.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!)**

- **DSS = DSA** (the algorithm named inside the FIPS 186 standard). Don't confuse the two.
- **Security basis:** Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) — *not* integer factorization (which is RSA).
- **Parameters (p, q, g):**
  - `p` is a large prime (1024–3072 bits).
  - `q` is a prime divisor of `p − 1` (160–256 bits).
  - `g` is a generator of the order-`q` subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.
- **Keys:** Private `x ∈ [1, q−1]`; Public `y = g^x mod p`.
- **Sign:** Pick random `k`. Compute `r = (g^k mod p) mod q`. Compute `s = k⁻¹(H(m) + x·r) mod q`. Output `(r, s)`.
- **Verify:** Compute `w = s⁻¹ mod q`, `u1 = H(m)·w mod q`, `u2 = r·w mod q`, `v = (g^{u1} · y^{u2} mod p) mod q`. Accept if `v = r`.
- **Range check `0 < r < q` and `0 < s < q`** is mandatory in verification.
- **`k` must be unique, unpredictable, and ideally deterministic (RFC 6979).** Never reuse `k` across signatures.
- **Three guarantees:** Authentication + Integrity + Non-repudiation.
- **Hash is mandatory:** Use SHA-256 (FIPS 186-4/5) — *never* MD5 or SHA-1 in production.
- **DSS does *not* provide confidentiality** — only the *signing* operation, not encryption.
- **Modern variants:** ECDSA (Bitcoin) and EdDSA (Ed25519) — same mathematical structure, different group (elliptic curve).
- **Library gotcha (PyCryptodome):** Use `DSA.generate(2048)` + `DSS.new(key, 'fips-186-3')` + `SHA256.new(msg)` — in this exact order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement LSB Steganography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCL507 - CRYPTOGRAPHY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement LSB Steganography # Module Experiment 2: Implement LSB Steganography ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steganography** is the art and science of concealing the very *existence* of a secret message within an ordinary, non-secret carrier medium (image, audio, video, or text), such that the carrier itself appears completely unmodified to an unauthorized observer. The word originates from Greek: *steganos* (covered) + *graphein* (to write). **LSB (Least Significant Bit) Steganography** is a spatial-domain image steganographic technique in which the secret message bits are embedded by replacing the *least significant bits* of the pixel intensity values in a digital cover image. The modification is performed on a per-pixel basis so that the resulting **stego-image** is visually indistinguishable from the original **cover image**. ### 1.2 Key Terminology Used in KTU Examinations | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Cover Image** | The original, innocent-looking image used as the carrier (denoted $C$) | | **Stego-Image** | The output image carrying the hidden message (denoted $S$) | | **Payload** | The secret message (text, image, or bits) to be hidden (denoted $M$) | | **Embedding Algorithm** | The function $E(C, M, K) \rightarrow S$ that hides the message | | **Extraction Algorithm** | The function $D(S, K) \rightarrow M$ that recovers the message | | **PSNR** | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio — measures imperceptibility in **dB** | | **MSE** | Mean Squared Error — measures pixel-level distortion | | **Capacity** | Maximum number of secret bits that can be embedded | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Ink on a Painting" Imagine a museum painting (the **cover image**) hanging on a wall. To the naked eye, every brushstroke is ordinary. However, the artist has used a special invisible ink (the **secret message**) on certain pixels. A tourist (the **unauthorized observer**) sees only the painting. A detective (the **recipient**) knows the exact pattern of brushstrokes that carry the ink and can extract the message by looking at the *last drop of paint* on each stroke — i.e., the **LSB**. The brilliant insight is this: the human eye cannot distinguish between, say, a pixel of intensity **154** and one of intensity **155** — a difference of just **1 unit out of 256**. Yet at the bit level, this 1-unit difference flips the last bit completely. This perceptual blindness of the Human Visual System (HVS) is the *entire foundation* of LSB steganography. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Least" Significant Bit?** In a **8-bit** grayscale image, bits contribute to intensity with weights $2^7, 2^6, 2^5, \ldots, 2^0$. The MSB ($2^7 = 128$) controls the dominant brightness; the LSB ($2^0 = 1$) controls only a 1-unit change. HVS is largely insensitive to changes of magnitude **≤ 2 units** in 256-level intensity, making the LSB the perfect hiding slot. ### 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Cryptography Lab Syllabus In **PCCCL507 — Cryptography Lab**, Experiment 2 introduces students to the practical implementation of spatial-domain information hiding. It bridges the gap between classical cryptography (where the *ciphertext* itself is visible) and steganography (where the *very act of communication* is concealed). Mastery of LSB is foundational for advanced experiments like **DCT-based steganography** and **audio LSB embedding**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Bit-Level Anatomy of a Pixel A standard 8-bit grayscale pixel $P$ can be expressed as a binary octet: $$ P = b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 $$ | Bit Position | Weight | Name | Role | |---|---|---|---| | $b_7$ | $2^7 = 128$ | MSB | Dominant intensity contributor | | $b_6$ | $2^6 = 64$ | — | Major brightness | | $b_5$ | $2^5 = 32$ | — | Major brightness | | $b_4$ | $2^4 = 16$ | — | Mid-tone | | $b_3$ | $2^3 = 8$ | — | Mid-tone | | $b_2$ | $2^2 = 4$ | — | Fine detail | | $b_1$ | $2^1 = 2$ | — | Fine detail | | $b_0$ | $2^0 = 1$ | **LSB** | **Message carrier** | In a **24-bit color image**, every pixel has three channels: **R, G, B**, each 8 bits. LSB substitution can therefore hide **3 bits per pixel** (one in R, one in G, one in B). ## 2.2 The 1-Bit LSB Embedding Algorithm (Logical Steps) > [!NOTE] > The following is the canonical LSB embedding procedure. **Memorize this flow** — it is the most frequently asked algorithm in KTU viva and lab exams. **Inputs:** Cover image $C$ of size $M \times N$, secret message $M$ (bit stream of length $L$). **Output:** Stego-image $S$. 1. Convert the cover image into a 1-D sequence of pixel intensities by **raster scanning** (left-to-right, top-to-bottom). 2. Convert the secret message into a binary bit stream (ASCII $\rightarrow$ 8-bit binary). 3. For each secret bit $m_i$ (where $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, L-1$): - Read the next cover pixel $C[i]$. - **Clear** its LSB: $C[i] = C[i] \;\texttt{AND}\; 254$ (binary `11111110`). - **Insert** the secret bit: $S[i] = C[i] \;\texttt{OR}\; m_i$. 4. Reshape the modified 1-D stream back into the $M \times N$ image to form $S$. ## 2.3 The 1-Bit LSB Extraction Algorithm 1. Read the stego-image $S$ as a 1-D sequence of pixels. 2. For each pixel $S[i]$, extract the LSB: $m_i = S[i] \;\texttt{AND}\; 1$. 3. Group every 8 extracted bits to form one character. 4. Continue until a pre-defined **delimiter** (e.g., `###END###` or null character) is reached. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symmetry Principle:** The extraction algorithm is the *exact inverse* of embedding. It does NOT need the original cover image — this property classifies LSB steganography as a **non-blind-invertible / blind extraction** scheme, which is a great viva point. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S_i = (C_i \;\&\; 254) \;\vert\; m_i$ | 1-bit LSB embedding (bitwise form) | dimensionless | | 2 | $S_i = C_i - (C_i \bmod 2) + m_i$ | Equivalent integer form of 1-bit LSB | dimensionless | | 3 | $m_i = S_i \bmod 2$ | Extraction of hidden bit | dimensionless | | 4 | $\text{Capacity}_{1\text{-bit}} = M \times N \times 3$ | Max bits in 24-bit RGB image | bits | | 5 | $\text{Capacity}_{1\text{-bit, gray}} = M \times N$ | Max bits in 8-bit grayscale image | bits | | 6 | $\text{Capacity}_{k\text{-bit}} = \lfloor L / k \rfloor$ | Pixels required to hide $L$ bits using $k$ LSBs | pixels | | 7 | $C = L \times \lceil 8 / k \rceil$ | Characters that fit in $L$ pixels (8 bits/char) | chars | | 8 | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N} [C(i,j) - S(i,j)]^2$ | Mean Squared Error | intensity$^2$ | | 9 | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{(\text{MAX})^2}{\text{MSE}} \right)$ | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **dB** | | 10 | $\text{MAX} = 2^b - 1$ (e.g., **255** for 8-bit) | Peak intensity | intensity | | 11 | $\text{PSNR} \geq 40$ dB | Threshold for **imperceptibility** | dB | | 12 | $\text{BPP} = \frac{\text{Embedded Bits}}{\text{Total Pixels}}$ | Bits Per Pixel (efficiency metric) | bits/pixel | > [!WARNING] > In your answer scripts, **never** write $PSNR = 10 \log \left( \frac{MAX^2}{MSE} \right)$ using the vertical bar `|x|` notation in a markdown table — it breaks the table parser. KTU digital evaluation scripts strictly enforce formula correctness, so use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ for modulus and absolute value. ## 2.5 Why the Formula Sheet Matters — Real-World Utility - **Digital Watermarking:** Copyright protection of images, where the watermark must survive JPEG compression and noise — PSNR > 40 dB is the industry watermark-quality threshold. - **Covert Military Communication:** LSB embedding in satellite imagery is used for low-bandwidth covert channels. - **Medical Imaging:** Patient identifiers can be hidden in MRI/CT scans for privacy-compliant transmission. - **Forensics & Steganalysis:** LSB is the *easiest* scheme to detect using **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) attack** and **RS Analysis** — students should mention this vulnerability in viva as a critical limitation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Bitwise Embedding Identity ### Step 1: Clear the LSB of the cover pixel The binary mask `11111110` has decimal value **254**. Performing bitwise AND with any 8-bit number $C_i$ forces the LSB to **0** while preserving all higher bits: $$ \begin{aligned} S_i^{\text{cleared}} &= C_i \;\&\; 254 \\ &= C_i \;\&\; (\text{11111110})_2 \\ &= b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 \mathbf{0} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Insert the secret bit The secret bit $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is a 1-bit value, so its decimal representation equals itself. Bitwise OR with $m_i$ writes the secret bit into the LSB slot: $$ \begin{aligned} S_i &= S_i^{\text{cleared}} \;\vert\; m_i \\ &= b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 \mathbf{m_i} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Equivalent integer form Since the LSB is the bit with weight $2^0 = 1$, the operation $C_i \bmod 2$ extracts it, and adding $m_i$ (which is 0 or 1) sets it: $$ S_i = C_i - (C_i \bmod 2) + m_i $$ > [!NOTE] > This integer form is the most exam-friendly representation. KTU examiners award full marks for this single-line expression when the steps are shown. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Embedding the Letter "A" (ASCII 65) **ASCII "A" = 65 = (01000001)$_2$ — eight secret bits.** **Step 1 — Secret bit stream:** $m = \{0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1\}$ **Step 2 — Cover pixels (selected from a grayscale image):** | Pixel Index $i$ | $C_i$ (decimal) | $C_i$ (binary) | $m_i$ | Cleared $C_i$ | $S_i$ (decimal) | $S_i$ (binary) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 154 | 10011010 | 0 | 10011010 | 154 | 10011010 | | 1 | 203 | 11001011 | 1 | 11001010 | 202 | 11001010 | | 2 | 87 | 01010111 | 0 | 01010110 | 86 | 01010110 | | 3 | 240 | 11110000 | 0 | 11110000 | 240 | 11110000 | | 4 | 12 | 00001100 | 0 | 00001100 | 12 | 00001100 | | 5 | 199 | 11000111 | 0 | 11000110 | 198 | 11000110 | | 6 | 65 | 01000001 | 0 | 01000000 | 64 | 01000000 | | 7 | 178 | 10110010 | 1 | 10110011 | 179 | 10110011 | **Step 3 — Verification:** Reading LSBs of $S$: $\{0,1,0,0,0,0,0,1\}$ → group into 8 bits → `01000001` → ASCII **65 = "A"** ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum change per pixel = ±1.** A pixel can change from 154 to 154 (no change) or from 154 to 155 (change of 1). It can NEVER jump by more than 1, which is why LSB is visually undetectable. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Capacity Formula For an image of $M \times N$ pixels with $C$ color channels (3 for RGB, 1 for grayscale), and $k$ LSBs used per channel: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total available bit slots} &= M \times N \times C \times k \\ \text{Max secret characters} &= \left\lfloor \frac{M \times N \times C \times k}{8} \right\rfloor \end{aligned} $$ **Sanity check for a $512 \times 512$ RGB image with 1 LSB:** $$ \text{Capacity} = \frac{512 \times 512 \times 3 \times 1}{8} = \frac{786432}{8} = 98{,}304 \text{ characters} \approx 98 \text{ KB} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation of the MSE and PSNR (with Full Algebra) ### Step 1 — Define the per-pixel squared error For each pixel position $(i, j)$: $$ E(i, j) = [C(i, j) - S(i, j)]^2 $$ Since LSB modification changes a pixel by **at most 1**, we have $E(i, j) \in \{0, 1\}$. ### Step 2 — Average over all pixels $$ \text{MSE} = \frac{1}{M \cdot N} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} E(i, j) $$ ### Step 3 — Convert MSE to PSNR The Peak Signal is the maximum possible intensity, $\text{MAX} = 2^b - 1 = 255$ for 8-bit images. PSNR is defined as the **logarithmic ratio of peak power to noise power**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PSNR} &= 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}} \right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255^2}{\text{MSE}} \right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}} \right) \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Worked PSNR Computation Suppose we embed 1000 secret bits in a $100 \times 100$ grayscale image and exactly 980 bits cause a ±1 change (i.e., 20 bits coincidentally match the original LSB, producing zero change). **Step 1 — Compute MSE:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MSE} &= \frac{1}{100 \times 100} \left[ 980 \times 1^2 + 20 \times 0^2 + (10000 - 1000) \times 0^2 \right] \\ &= \frac{980}{10000} = 0.098 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute PSNR:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PSNR} &= 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255^2}{0.098} \right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{65025}{0.098} \right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10} (663520.4) \\ &= 10 \times 5.8218 \\ &\approx 58.21 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > A PSNR of **58.21 dB** is far above the 40 dB imperceptibility threshold, confirming the visual indistinguishability of the stego-image. This kind of numerical problem is a **favourite KTU Part B question**. ## 3.6 The k-Bit LSB Generalization If $k$ least significant bits are used per pixel, the embedding becomes: $$ S_i = (C_i \;\&\; (\sim((2^k) - 1))) \;\vert\; (m_i \;\&\; ((2^k) - 1)) $$ The capacity grows linearly with $k$, but so does the visual distortion. Typical trade-offs: | $k$ | Capacity (RGB, $512 \times 512$) | Typical PSNR | Imperceptibility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 98,304 bytes | ~52 dB | **Excellent** | | 2 | 196,608 bytes | ~46 dB | Good | | 3 | 294,912 bytes | ~40 dB | Marginal | | 4 | 393,216 bytes | ~34 dB | Visible artifacts | > [!WARNING] > Above $k = 4$, the human eye begins to detect patchy textures and grayscale banding. Examiners may deduct marks if you recommend $k > 4$ without justification. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between cryptography and steganography. State one advantage of steganography over cryptography.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Cryptography | Steganography | |---|---|---| | Goal | Scrambles message into unreadable form | Hides the *existence* of the message | | Output | Ciphertext (visibly encrypted) | Innocent carrier (visibly unmodified) | | Attacker knows | A secret exists | Nothing unusual is happening | | Detection resistance | Low (ciphertext is obvious) | High (carrier looks normal) | | Combined use | Provides confidentiality | Provides *undetectability* | **Advantage:** Steganography conceals the very *fact* that communication is taking place, while cryptography only conceals the *content*. In hostile environments, the absence of visible ciphertext prevents an adversary from even attempting cryptanalysis. > [!NOTE] > Examiners award full marks only if the answer mentions *both* a clear difference **and** a concrete advantage. A one-line "steganography is better" answer will score ≤ 1 mark. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define LSB. Why is the LSB chosen for hiding data instead of the MSB?** **Model Answer:** The **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** of a binary number is the bit with the lowest positional weight ($2^0 = 1$ in an 8-bit system). Modifying the LSB alters a pixel's intensity by **at most 1 unit out of 256**, which is below the perceptual threshold of the Human Visual System (HVS). **Why LSB and not MSB?** The MSB carries weight $2^7 = 128$. Flipping the MSB changes the pixel by up to **128 units**, producing instantly visible bright/dark spots and destroying the carrier's appearance. LSB modifications are essentially invisible, making them the only viable single-bit hiding slot. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-based questions carry 14 marks with an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **LSB embedding algorithm** to hide a text message in a grayscale image. State the formulas used. (7 Marks) **(b)** A $256 \times 256$ grayscale cover image is used to hide a 200-character message using 2-bit LSB substitution. Compute (i) the **embedding capacity** in characters, and (ii) the **maximum possible MSE** if all embedded pixels incur a change of exactly 3. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to (a) The embedding process consists of three primary stages: 1. **Message Conversion:** Convert each character of the text into its 8-bit ASCII representation to form a 1-D bit stream $m_0, m_1, \ldots, m_{L-1}$ where $L = 8 \times (\text{message length in characters})$. 2. **Pixel Iteration:** Raster-scan the cover image $C$ of size $M \times N$ to produce a 1-D pixel sequence $C_0, C_1, \ldots, C_{MN-1}$. 3. **Bit Substitution:** For each secret bit $m_i$: $$ S_i = (C_i \;\&\; 254) \;\vert\; m_i \quad \equiv \quad S_i = C_i - (C_i \bmod 2) + m_i $$ 4. **Reshape:** Reassemble the 1-D stego-stream $S$ back into an $M \times N$ 2-D image. **Block diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete pipeline schematic. **Helper metadata** (length of message) is usually stored in the first 32 pixels of the stego-image so the receiver knows how many bits to read. #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** $M = N = 256$, $k = 2$ LSBs, message length = 200 characters. **(i) Embedding Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total pixels} &= 256 \times 256 = 65{,}536 \\ \text{Bits per pixel} &= 2 \\ \text{Total bit slots} &= 65{,}536 \times 2 = 131{,}072 \text{ bits} \\ \text{Total characters} &= \frac{131{,}072}{8} = 16{,}384 \text{ characters} \end{aligned} $$ Since the message is only 200 characters $= 1600$ bits, it occupies: $$ \text{Pixels needed} = \frac{1600}{2} = 800 \text{ pixels} $$ **Capacity utilization** = $\frac{200}{16{,}384} \times 100\% \approx 1.22\%$ **(ii) Maximum Possible MSE:** With 2-bit LSB, a pixel can change by up to $2^k - 1 = 3$ units. The worst case is when all 800 modified pixels incur the maximum error of 3 (i.e., $E(i,j) = 3^2 = 9$), and the remaining $65{,}536 - 800 = 64{,}736$ pixels are unchanged ($E = 0$): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MSE}_{\max} &= \frac{1}{M \cdot N} \sum_{i,j} E(i,j) \\ &= \frac{1}{65{,}536} \left[ 800 \times 9 + 64{,}736 \times 0 \right] \\ &= \frac{7200}{65{,}536} \\ &\approx 0.1099 \end{aligned} $$ **Corresponding PSNR:** $$ \text{PSNR}_{\min} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{0.1099}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(591{,}901.7) \approx 57.72 \text{ dB} $$ This is comfortably above the **40 dB** imperceptibility threshold. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **extraction algorithm** for recovering a hidden message from a 1-bit LSB stego-image. Why is the original cover image NOT required at the receiver? (7 Marks) **(b)** A stego-image is reported to have $\text{MSE} = 0.05$. Compute the **PSNR** in dB and comment on its **imperceptibility**. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to (a) **Extraction Algorithm (1-bit LSB):** 1. Read the stego-image $S$ of size $M \times N$ and raster-scan it into a 1-D pixel stream. 2. For each pixel $S_i$, recover the embedded bit using: $$ m_i = S_i \bmod 2 \quad \equiv \quad m_i = S_i \;\&\; 1 $$ 3. Group every 8 consecutive recovered bits to reconstruct one ASCII character. 4. Continue until the pre-agreed **delimiter** (e.g., `###END###`) is found. **Why the cover image is not required:** The LSB embedding formula $S_i = (C_i \;\&\; 254) \;\vert\; m_i$ writes $m_i$ *directly* into the LSB slot of $S_i$. The extraction formula $m_i = S_i \bmod 2$ reads *only* the LSB slot. Since higher bits of $C_i$ are not involved in extraction, the receiver never needs to know the original pixel value $C_i$. This makes LSB a **blind extraction** scheme, which is a major practical advantage (saves bandwidth and storage). > [!NOTE] > This "blindness" property is a *favourite viva question*. Answer with the bitwise reasoning, not just "because it is designed that way." #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** $\text{MSE} = 0.05$, $\text{MAX} = 255$ (standard 8-bit image). **Step 1 — Substitute into the PSNR formula:** $$ \text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{0.05}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Compute the numerator:** $$ 255^2 = 65{,}025 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the ratio:** $$ \frac{65{,}025}{0.05} = 1{,}300{,}500 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the logarithm:** $$ \text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(1{,}300{,}500) $$ $$ \log_{10}(1{,}300{,}500) = \log_{10}(1.3005 \times 10^6) = 6 + \log_{10}(1.3005) \approx 6 + 0.1141 = 6.1141 $$ **Step 5 — Final answer:** $$ \text{PSNR} = 10 \times 6.1141 \approx \mathbf{61.14 \text{ dB}} $$ **Comment on Imperceptibility:** Since **61.14 dB ≫ 40 dB**, the stego-image is **highly imperceptible** — the human eye cannot distinguish it from the original cover. This indicates that very few pixel changes occurred (low MSE), or that the changes were uniformly distributed. Such a high PSNR is typical of well-designed 1-bit or 2-bit LSB schemes. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-deduction traps in LSB Steganography questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the LSB in the formula:** Writing $S_i = C_i + m_i$ instead of $S_i = C_i - (C_i \bmod 2) + m_i$ is WRONG and loses 2–3 marks. The LSB must be *cleared first*, not added blindly. > 2. **Unit errors in PSNR:** PSNR is ALWAYS in **decibels (dB)**. Writing "PSNR = 58" without "dB" is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Capacity miscounting:** Students often forget the factor of 3 for RGB images. For a $256 \times 256$ grayscale image with 1 LSB, capacity is $65{,}536$ bits, **not** $65{,}536 \times 3$. > 4. **Confusing MSE and PSNR:** MSE is in intensity$^2$ (no log), PSNR is in dB (log applied). Examiners check this carefully. > 5. **Skipping the delimiter discussion:** In extraction algorithm answers, ALWAYS mention the end-of-message marker. Without it, the receiver cannot determine where the hidden message terminates. > 6. **Viva trap — "Is LSB secure?"** The correct answer is **NO**. LSB is fragile, easily detected by $\chi^2$ steganalysis, and destroyed by any lossy compression (e.g., JPEG). Examiners use this to test depth of understanding. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-frequency viva + exam revision points — read this section 30 minutes before the lab exam.** - ✅ **Full form of LSB** = *Least Significant Bit*; weight $2^0 = 1$ in an 8-bit system. - ✅ **Maximum change per pixel** in 1-bit LSB = $\pm 1$ intensity unit. - ✅ **Core embedding identity (memorize verbatim):** $S_i = (C_i \;\&\; 254) \;\vert\; m_i$ - ✅ **Core extraction identity (memorize verbatim):** $m_i = S_i \bmod 2$ - ✅ **Capacity formulas:** Grayscale 1-LSB $\rightarrow M \times N$ bits; RGB 1-LSB $\rightarrow 3 \times M \times N$ bits. - ✅ **PSNR threshold for imperceptibility:** $\geq$ **40 dB** (industry standard). - ✅ **MAX value for 8-bit images:** **255**. - ✅ **PSNR formula (no mistakes):** $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{MAX}^2 / \text{MSE})$ — always in **dB**. - ✅ **Blind extraction:** Original cover is *not* required at the receiver (key advantage). - ✅ **Delimiter / EOF marker** is essential to mark the end of the hidden message. - ✅ **Raster-scan order:** Row-major (left-to-right, top-to-bottom) is the standard. - ✅ **Vulnerability:** LSB is detectable via **$\chi^2$ attack**, **RS analysis**, and **Sample Pair Analysis**. - ✅ **Fragility:** Any lossy compression (JPEG) or format conversion destroys the LSB payload. - ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher $k$ (more LSBs) $\Rightarrow$ higher capacity but lower PSNR (more visible). - ✅ **KTU viva classic:** "Name two techniques to improve LSB security" → answer: (1) encryption of the message *before* embedding, (2) randomized pixel selection using a **stego-key**.
